coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of multicolor emitting sulfur quantum dots and their applications in light blocking field, anti-counterfeiting and sensing
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Xiule
Yan, Fanyong
Xu, Ming
Ning, Jin
Wei, Xin
Bai, Xinyi
coredata.dc:description: Sulfur quantum dots (SQDs) have aroused widespread interest from researchers in a wide range of fields due to their excellent photoluminescent properties. Ethylenediamine, diaminopropane and butanediamine were used as precursor amine raw materials to interact with sublimated sulfur to synthesize SQDs with blue, cyan and green fluorescence emission, respectively. Multicolour emitting SQDs were first prepared via sulfur-amine interactions. Further characterization and calculations showed that the precursor amine substances could alter growth size and band gap energy of SQDs to allow for a wider absorption and fluorescence transfer to long wavelength emission region, resulting in tunable fluorescence emission. In terms of application, the excellent down-conversion properties of SQDs were utilized to obtain highly transparent and flexible photoblocking films by blending SQDs with polyvinyl alcohol matrixes, achieving a blocking of light in UV region of up to 99.69 %. In addition, we constructed an encoded storage microarray based on standard 8-bit ASCII character binary codes using BSQDs and GSQDs to store and encrypt important information. Finally, GSQDs-based fluorescent sensors were designed to achieve fluorescent trace detection of o-nitrophenols with limits of detection as low as 2.54 μM. The multicolor emitting SQDs prepared in this work have great potential for applications in analytical detection, optical anti-counterfeiting and light blocking.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sulfur quantum dots
Nanomaterials
Multicolor emission
Optical anti-counterfeiting
Sensing

Application of SQDs/PVA composite films in light blocking
Applications of SQDs in anti-counterfeit encryption
Application of GSQDs in sensing detection
Determination of ONP in practical samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51678409), State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes (Z1-201507), and the Program for Innovative Research Team in University of Tianjin (TD13-5042). We would like to thank the Analytical & Testing Center of Tiangong University for Transmission Electron Microscope, X-Ray Diffractometer, X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of BSQDs (a), CSQDs (c) and GSQDs (e). Inset: HRETM images of SQDs. Size distribution of BSQDs (b), CSQDs (d) and GSQDs (f).

UV–Vis spectra, fluorescence spectra and electron photographs of BSQDs (a), CSQDs (c) and GSQDs (e) at daylight and UV lamp (365 nm). Fluorescence emission spectra of BSQDs (b), CSQDs (d) and GSQDs (f).
XPS spectra of BSQDs (a), CSQDs (c) and GSQDs (e), S2p spectra of BSQDs (b), CSQDs (d) and GSQDs (f).
(a–c) XRD spectra of BSQDs, CSQDs and GSQDs. (d) Raman spectra of BSQDs, CSQDs and GSQDs.

(a–c) Emission spectral intensities of UV-LED lamp blocked by three SQDs/PVA composite films, (d) Light blocking ratios of the composite films at different SQDs loadings, (e) Digital photographs of UV-LED lamp blocked by GSQDs/PVA composite films.

(a–c) Normalized fluorescence emission spectra of GSQDs/PVA composite films at excitation wavelengths of 350, 400 and 450 nm. (d) Transmittance spectra of GSQDs/PVA composite films at different wavelengths. (e) Digital photographs of the letters “TGU” taken through GSQDs/PVA composite films under sunlight. (f) Digital photographs of UV test cards irradiated by UV LED lamps. (g) Digital photographs of UV test cards irradiated under UV LED lamps through pure PVA films. (h) Digital photographs of UV test cards irradiated under UV LED lamps through GSQDs/PVA composite films.
Information encryption applications of three SQDs. (a) Binary code, (b) numerical code written on filter paper with three SQDs under sunlight and UV light (365 nm), respectively.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of GSQDs at different ONP concentrations (1–300 µM), inset: photographs of GSQDs at different ONP concentrations in daylight (top) and 365 nm UV light (bottom). (b) Linear regression curve of F/F 0 on ONP concentration (1–240 µM). (c) Selective detection of ONP. (d) Competitive detection of ONP. (e) Fluorescence spectra of GSQDs and UV–Vis spectra of ONP. (f) UV–Vis spectra of GSQDs, ONP and GSQDs + ONP.
Diagram of the fabrication process for multicolor emitting SQDs and the mechanism of their applications.
Determination of ONP in dye wastewater and pesticide wastewater samples by GSQDs.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of multicolor emitting sulfur quantum dots and their applications in light blocking field, anti-counterfeiting and sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Textiles Science and Engineering, Tiangong University, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Textiles Science and Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Tiangong University, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Diagram of the fabrication process for multicolor emitting SQDs and the mechanism of their applications.

Sulfur quantum dots (SQDs) have aroused widespread interest from researchers in a wide range of fields due to their excellent photoluminescent properties. Ethylenediamine, diaminopropane and butanediamine were used as precursor amine raw materials to interact with sublimated sulfur to synthesize SQDs with blue, cyan and green fluorescence emission, respectively. Multicolour emitting SQDs were first prepared via sulfur-amine interactions. Further characterization and calculations showed that the precursor amine substances could alter growth size and band gap energy of SQDs to allow for a wider absorption and fluorescence transfer to long wavelength emission region, resulting in tunable fluorescence emission. In terms of application, the excellent down-conversion properties of SQDs were utilized to obtain highly transparent and flexible photoblocking films by blending SQDs with polyvinyl alcohol matrixes, achieving a blocking of light in UV region of up to 99.69 %. In addition, we constructed an encoded storage microarray based on standard 8-bit ASCII character binary codes using BSQDs and GSQDs to store and encrypt important information. Finally, GSQDs-based fluorescent sensors were designed to achieve fluorescent trace detection of o-nitrophenols with limits of detection as low as 2.54 μM. The multicolor emitting SQDs prepared in this work have great potential for applications in analytical detection, optical anti-counterfeiting and light blocking.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: Sulfur is abundant in nature as by-product of natural gas purification and refining process. However, it is inherently highly flammable, which will lead to serious environmental pollution and resource waste problems unless it is used rationally [1]. The fabrication of fluorescent sulfur quantum dots (SQDs) is one of the ways to utilize the use of byproduct sulfur [2,3]. SQDs are originally prepared by a complex and tedious multi-step process [4]. Recently improved preparation processes are developed to synthesize SQDs in one step using NaOH treatment of sublimated sulfur and polyethylene glycol (PEG) as a passivating agent [5,6]. Based on this study, researchers have continued to optimize this synthesis method, including the use of H2O2 assisted etching [7], optimizations of the reaction atmosphere (O2) [8], alteration of the reaction conditions [9], and replacement of the passivation agents [10].

However, almost all reported methods utilized the S/NaOH reaction system for the preparation of SQDs [11,12]. This reaction system was time-consuming and required additional passivation agents, which greatly limited the practical application of SQDs [13]. Since the nitrogen in amine solvents enabled directly perform nucleophilic assault to disrupt the steady octa-atomic ring of sulfur, resulting in the creation of long-chain sulfur radical ions of excellent solubility. Thus, sulfur could be dissolved in amine solvents in large quantities. Based on this property of sulfur in amine solvents, amine solvents should be more suitable than NaOH for handling sublimated sulfur to facilitate the preparation of SQDs. In our previous work, a novel ethylenediamine-assisted acceleration strategy was developed to reduce synthesis time of SQDs to 6 h [14]. The synthesized SQDs exhibited reasonable photoluminescence quantum yields (QY) up to 14.22 % and full width at half maximum (FWHM) down to 74 nm. Unfortunately, the fluorescence emission of the SQDs synthesized by this strategy was concentrated in the blue spectral region [6]. Despite the reports on green fluorescence-emitting SQDs, few researchers have applied them due to the reported poor fluorescence performance of green-emitting SQDs [7]. Therefore, it was necessary to develop high-performance long-wavelength emitting multicolor SQDs [15]. Furthermore, the application of SQDs was limited mostly to the field of analytical sensing [16]. Therefore broadening the range of SQDs applications was also an issue that needed to be addressed at present [17].

In this work, multicolor emitting SQDs were produced only by changing the precursor amine for the synthesis of SQDs. Blue-emitting SQDs (BSQDs) were obtained when ethylenediamine (EDA) was used as the precursor amine. Cyan-emitting SQDs (CSQDs) were obtained when diaminopropane (PDA) was used as the precursor amine, and green-emitting SQDs (GSQDs) were obtained when butanediamine (BDA) was used as the precursor amine. It was demonstrated by further characterization and calculations that the precursor amine substances could change the growth size and band gap energy of SQDs to enable a broader absorption and fluorescence transfer to the long wavelength emission region, leading to tunable fluorescence emission. Taking advantage of the excellent down-conversion properties of SQDs, highly transparent and flexible photoblocking films were obtained by mixing SQDs with polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) substrates to achieve the blocking of light in the UV region. This blocking effect did not affect the transmission of visible light. In addition, a coded storage microarray based on standard 8-bit ASCII character binary codes using BSQDs and GSQDs was constructed to store and encrypt important information. Finally, a fluorescence sensor based on GSQDs was designed to achieve fluorescence trace detection of ONP (Scheme 1 ).

To the flask, sublimated sulfur (0.6 g), 1, 2-ethylenediamine (EDA) (3 mL) and EtOH (20 mL) were added sequentially and refluxed for 6 h at 70 °C. Upon cooling, 5 mL above suspension was removed and 5 mL H2O2 (7.75 wt%) were placed droplet by droplet under agitation. Then the suspension became clear. The resulting solution was dialyzed, freeze-dried to remove the solvent to produce BSQDs, and stored in a refrigerator (4 °C).

To the flask, sublimated sulfur (0.6 g), 1, 3-diaminopropane (PDA) and EtOH (20 mL) were added sequentially and refluxed for 24 h at 70 °C. Upon cooling, 5 mL above suspension was removed and 7 mL H2O2 (7.75 wt%) were placed droplet by droplet under agitation. Then the suspension became clear. The resulting solution was dialyzed, freeze-dried to remove the solvent to produce BSQDs, and stored in a refrigerator (4 °C).

To the flask, sublimated sulfur (0.6 g), 1, 4-butanediamine (BDA) and EtOH (20 mL) were added sequentially and refluxed for 24 h at 70 °C. Upon cooling, 5 mL above suspension was removed and 7 mL H2O2 (7.75 wt%) were placed droplet by droplet under agitation. Then the suspension became clear. The resulting solution was dialyzed, freeze-dried to remove the solvent to produce BSQDs, and stored in a refrigerator (4 °C).

Without adding any precursor amine substances, sublimated sulfur as the sulfur source were added sequentially and refluxed for 6 h at 70 °C. After etching with the same concentration and volume of H2O2, the solution was almost non-fluorescent. This demonstrated the effect of precursor amine substances on the optical properties of SQDs. It eliminated the possibility of fluorescence due to side reactions during the synthesis of SQDs.

Mix PVA (3 g) and distilled water (70 mL) and stir until a clear solution was obtained. The above appropriate amount of PVA solution was added into 9 beakers and a certain amount of aqueous BSQDs (5 g/L) was added to them to prepare BSQDs/PVA mixtures with different contents (0, 3.2, 6.4, 9.6, 12.8, 16.0, 19.2, 22.4, 25.6 wt%), and the blends were agitated for 1 h to obtain clarified and homogeneous solutions. The solutions were dropped into 3.2 cm × 3.2 cm square molds and dried at 60 °C for 10 h to prepare highly transparent and flexible BSQDs/PVA composite films. The above experimental steps were repeated by replacing the BSQDs with CSQDs and GSQDs to prepare CSQDs/PVA composite films and GSQDs/PVA composite films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical sensing of ONP by GSQDs
ce:section.ce:para: 3 g/L GSQDs solutions were prepared with distilled water as solvent. 150 μL of GSQDs solution was added to 10 mL volumetric flasks, followed by various amounts of ONP solution (2 mM) and the above solution was diluted to 10 mL. Then the fluorescence spectral results were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 420 nm. To circumvent errors, the above tests were performed thrice.

The shape and particle dimensions of three SQDs were recorded. TEM images revealed that three SQDs were homogeneous and monodisperse with a spherical shape. However, they displayed different size distributions of 2.3–4.8 nm (Fig. 1 a), 4.2–7.0 nm (Fig. 1c), and 6.3–8.1 nm (Fig. 1e), with average diameters of 3.6 nm (Fig. 1b), 5.6 nm (Fig. 1d), and 7.2 nm (Fig. 1f), respectively. The standard deviations of their three particle sizes were 0.5 nm, 0.4 nm and 0.6 nm. In addition, SQDs had a lattice fringe spacing of 0.23 nm. The images of HRTEM further demonstrated the successful synthesis of SQDs. This indicated the growth size of SQDs could be associated with the raw amine involved in synthesis.

The UV–vis spectra and fluorescence spectra of BSQDs, CSQDs and GSQDs were measured to study the optical properties of them (Fig. 2 a, c, e). Sublimated sulfur was rapidly converted from a stable octatomic ring to a long-chain sulfur radical ion in the presence of precursor amines. They were unstable at high temperature and continue to fracture to form the sulfur-nitrogen compound S4N4. And eventually they were oxidized to SQDs with oxygen vacancies [8]. SQDs with oxygen vacancies promoted the radiative relaxation of sulfur by providing additional energy states in the blue, cyan, and green spectral regions, resulting in intense blue, cyan, and green fluorescence, respectively. BSQDs, CSQDs and GSQDs all had strong absorption at 197 nm. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensities of the three SQDs under different pH, time, temperature and salt concentration conditions proved that they have excellent photostability (Fig. S1). In general, atomic sulfur contains non-bonded electrons and may undergo an n → σ* transition in the 150–250 nm range [4]. Therefore, the absorption at 197 nm was ascribed to the n → σ* transition of sulfur. The tail band monitored between 250 and 270 nm may be ascribed to the n → π* transition of sulfur atoms. GSQDs showed a weak absorption peak at 350 nm attributed to a direct band gap transition of S[0], suggesting the existence of oxidation reactions from Sx 2− to S[0] (Fig. 2e) [8]. The precursor amine substance had an effect on the quantum effect, making the electron behavior discrete. The distance between the electron energy levels changed, leading to bandgap transitions in SQDs. The fluorescence spectra of three SQDs at different excitation wavelengths showed that 360 nm, 370 nm and 420 nm were the optimal excitation wavelengths for BSQDs, CSQDs and GSQDs. And the corresponding emission wavelengths were centered at 435 nm, 456 nm and 515 nm, respectively (Fig. 2b, d, f). The three SQDs exhibited excitation-independent emission characteristics. Owing to short chain amine-containing organics passivating the surface states of the SQDs, their emission could only occur via the n → σ* transition from sulfur in a single leap mode with a certain energy [17], resulting in independence of excitation [18]. The three SQDs were clarified homogeneously transparent in daylight and showed intense blue, cyan and green fluorescence at UV lamp (365 nm), respectively.

The quantum yield of SQDs could be obtained through two approaches. Firstly, it could be obtained by direct measurement using a steady-state transient fluorescence spectrometer. This was how the quantum yields of SQDs are obtained in this paper. Secondly, the fluorescence QY of SQDs was calculated by Equation (1). The relevant calculations were performed using quinine sulfate as the reference substance. Where n was the refractive index of the solvent; K was the slope of the linear fit curve of the integrated fluorescence intensity and absorbance. And the subscripts R and S represented the reference and sample, respectively. The QY of BSQDs, CSQDs and GSQDs were finalized as 14.22 %, 13.89 % and 1.87 %. Only a portion of the energy of GSQDs could be converted into fluorescent photons, and the rest of energy was dissipated in other forms. Therefore GSQDs had a low QY. Nevertheless lower fluorescent QY has no effect on its performance in the field of photo-blocking, anti-counterfeit encryption and sensing.
(1)
Q Y =
Q
Y R
∙
K S
∙
n
S
2
K R
∙
n
R
2
× 100 %

The structural characterizations of SQDs were explored by FT-IR, Raman and XPS spectra, respectively. The absorption peaks at 3558 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of –OH and –NH2 [19] in FT-IR spectra (Fig. S2d). The peaks at 1627 cm−1, 1477 cm−1, 1195 cm−1, 1110 cm−1, 985 cm−1, and 619 cm−1 were attributed to the NH bending vibration, SO asymmetric stretching vibration, SO symmetric stretching vibration, COH stretching vibration, SNS stretching vibration, and SO bending vibration, respectively [20–22]. The existence of SN bonds showed that the sublimated sulfur reacted chemically with EDA. The amine groups broke the steady octa-atomic ring in S8 and this breakup was progressively intensified with reactions so that the main body sulphur was split to super-small nanodots [13,14,23]. The Raman spectra of the three SQDs were illustrated in Fig. 4
d. The peak at 975 cm−1 was derived from the stretching vibration of the v1 mode of the sulfate group [24]. The peak at 446 cm−1 originated from the SS stretching vibration of sulfur [22,25].

Next, the XPS spectra of the three SQDs were analyzed (Fig. 3). BSQDs, CSQDs and GSQDs contained S, C, N and O elements, and the content ratios of S, C, N and O were 1:2.87:1.95:3.21, 1:2.40:1.54:3.24 and 1:3.97:1.83:3.16, respectively. The large abundance of N elements confirmed the possible presence of amino groups on SQDs surface, in agreement with the observations from the FT-IR spectra. The S2p spectra for the three SQDs included five sulfur binding energies (Fig. 3b–f). The binding energy peaks at 169.50 eV, 169.00 eV and 168.00 eV were attributed to SO3 2- (2p1/2), SO2 2- (2p1/2) and SO2 - (2p2/3), respectively [25,26]. This was consistent with the results shown by Raman spectra (Fig. 4d). The binding energy peaks at 161.40 eV, 162.70 eV and 163.30 eV were attributed to atomic sulfur (S [0]) [11]. Fig. 4 showed the XRD spectra of three SQDs. BSQDs showed XRD spectra similar to S8 (PDF#83–2285) and S11 (PDF#78-0792), CSQDs showed XRD spectra similar to S (PDF#78-0793) and S6S10 (PDF#77-0228), and GSQDs showed XRD spectra similar to S18 (PDF#72-0409). The above results indicated that three SQDs formed different crystalline sulfur polycrystalline phases.

Constructing Tauc plots to measure the band gap energies of three SQDs to investigate their fluorescence colour variations (Fig. S3). The band gap energies of BSQDs, CSQDs and GSQDs were calculated to 5.81 eV, 5.60 eV and 5.50 eV, respectively [27]. Their values decreased as the size of the SQDs increased, indicated band gap energies were size dependent. The energy gaps of three SQDs were getting narrower, resulted in their wider absorption and fluorescence transfer to the long wavelength emission region, which was responsible for the change of their fluorescence emission wavelengths (Fig. S2e) [28].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of SQDs/PVA composite films in light blocking
ce:section.ce:para: Our eyes are frequently subjected to UV light from the sun or electronic devices, causing significant negative effects on our eyes [29,30]. Take advantage of the excellent down-conversion properties of SQDs. SQDs/PVA composite films were prepared for absorbing UV light and down-converting them for longer wavelengths in the visible range. For studying the light absorption and wavelength conversion capabilities of three SQDs/PVA composite films, we tested the emission spectral intensities of three SQDs/PVA composite films (0–25.6 wt% content of SQDs) when used to block light from LEDs emitting at a wavelength of 375 nm (Fig. 5 a–c). The pure PVA films had no blocking effect on the light of 375 nm LED. The light blocking efficacy increased as the increasing mass fraction of SQDs in the film. When the content of SQDs in PVA films reached 25.6 wt%, the light blocking rates of 375 nm LEDs were 87.29 %, 93.60 % and 99.69 % for BSQDs/PVA, CSQDs/PVA and GSQDs/PVA composite films, respectively (Fig. 5d). The GSQDs/PVA composite film had higher blocking rates than the BSQDs/PVA and CSQDs/PVA composite films under the same load, attributed to stronger and wider absorption of UV light by GSQDs.

Next, photographs of the GSQDs/PVA composite films loaded under different loads of GSQDs (0–25.6 wt%) placed in front of a 375 nm LEDs were taken to further evaluate the UV light blocking ability of them (Fig. 5e). With the pure PVA films placed, the strength of transmitted UV light was comparable to that without the films, indicating that the pure PVA films had no blocking ability to UV light. When the loading of GSQDs was 3.2–12.8 wt%, the strength of transmitted UV light was weakening with the increase loading of GSQDs, because most of the UV light was absorbed by GSQDs. When the loading of GSQDs was 16.0 wt% or more, the UV light blocking rate is nearly 100 %.

The optical characteristics of GSQDs/PVA composite films were characterized by fluorescence emission spectra, and the wavelength conversion ability of GSQDs/PVA composite films in absorbing UV light was also interpreted (Fig. 6 a–c). The emission of GSQDs/PVA composite films were centered at 460, 490, and 520 nm when the excitation wavelengths were 350, 400, and 450 nm, respectively. This indicated that the UV blocking mechanism of the composite films was based on the absorption of light at short wavelengths and the down-conversion of them to longer wavelengths of light [31]. Next, the transmission spectra of the composite films were studied to evaluate the light transmission ability of them at different wavelengths (Fig. 6d). The pure PVA films showed high transmittance in the UV region, but the transmittance of the composite films in the UV region gradually dropped with increasing the amount of loaded GSQDs, indicating that they strongly absorb light in the UV region. When the concentration of GSQDs reached 25.6 wt%, the transmittance of composite films in UV region were zero, which demonstrated the excellent UV light blocking ability of them. However, the UV blocking ability of the composite films did not affect the visible region, and they achieved more than 75 % transmission of visible light even when loaded with GSQDs at a concentration of 25.6 wt%. Fig. 6e showed digital photos of the letters “TGU” taken under sunlight through the GSQDs/PVA composite films. The writing was still visible through the composite films, demonstrating the excellent transmittance of visible light of them.

The UV blocking capacity of the composite films was tested using commercially available UV test cards. When the UV test card was irradiated with UV LED lamp, the irradiated area turned blue (Fig. 6f). The pure PVA films were placed in front of the test card, and the irradiated area remained blue after the UV LED lamp irradiation (Fig. 6g). When the GSQDs/PVA composite films were placed in front of the test card, the area of the test card covered by the composite films did not change color after the UV LED lamp irradiation (Fig. 6h), indicating that the UV light was blocked and absorbed. The comparison with the UV barrier of other materials was added to Table S1. All these results proved the excellent UV blocking ability of the composite films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of SQDs in anti-counterfeit encryption
ce:section.ce:para: The potential applications of three SQDs in anti-counterfeit encryption were explored. We designed a coded storage microarray based on standard 8-bit ASCII character binary codes. BSQDs “ink” and GSQDs “ink” were used to represent “0″ and ”1″, respectively. As shown in Fig. 7 a, all markers were colorless in daylight and could not be read by the naked eye as valid information. When the UV lamp was turned on, the markers made by the BSQDs “ink” and GSQDs “ink” showed bright blue and green fluorescence. The hidden codes could be recognized and decoded as “01010100″, ”01000111″ and “01010101″, which correspond to the capital letters ”T“, ”G“ and ”U“ based on ASCII binary codes. In addition, numeric codes (e.g. code ”2023″) were written on the filter paper using the three SQDs “inks”. The numerical codes could only be identified under UV light (Fig. 7b). Clearly, the above results showed that the three SQDs could be used in the field of anti-counterfeit encryption. Shining a UV lamp on the soapy-washed substrate revealed that the numerical code is still accurately recognizable (Fig.S4). It proved the stability of the film on substrate, and SQDs had a broad application prospect in the field of information encryption.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of GSQDs in sensing detection
ce:section.ce:para: The application of GSQDs in sensing detection was explored. We conducted sensing experiments using o-nitrophenol (ONP), p-nitrophenol (PNP), m-nitrophenol (MNP), 2,4-dinitrophenol and picric acid. It could be found that all three quench the fluorescence of GSQDs, but they had different degrees of quenching. The degree of quench was ranked as, ONP > PNP > MNP > 2,4-dinitrophenol > picric acid (Fig. S5a). Secondly, the UV spectra showed that GSQDs had spectral overlap with ONP, PNP, MNP, 2,4-dinitrophenol and picric acid. Among them, the most overlap was with ONP, while the less overlap was with PNP, MNP, 2,4-dinitrophenol and picric acid (Fig. S5b). As a result, we selected ONP as the target to be detected in sensing experiments. ONP was widely used in the manufacture of dyes and pesticides, etc. Due to its high toxicity at low concentrations, it has caused serious adverse effects on plants and animals and was classified as a priority toxic pollutant. It is crucial to develop a method for trace detection of ONP [32]. A fluorescent sensor based on GSQDs was designed to achieve fluorescent trace detection of ONP. Under 420 nm excitation, the fluorescence intensity of GSQDs at 515 nm diminished as the ONP concentration varied from 1 to 300 μM (Fig. 8 a). The fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F 0) showed a good linear relationship with ONP concentration (1–240 μM) (Fig. 8b). The fitting equation was F/F 0 = −0.00358C + 0.99549 (R 2 = 0.9993). Limits of detection down to 2.54 μM (based on 3σ/K), where C was the concentration of ONP, F and F 0 were the fluorescence intensities of GSQDs with and without ONP presence, respectively.

BSQDs, CSQDs and GSQDs all have strong light absorption. However, from the UV–Vis absorption spectra, it is clear that GSQDs have the widest range of light absorption. Therefore there are more substances that have absorption in this wavelength range. In contrast, GSQDs can specifically detect o-nitrophenol (ONP), indicating that the sensor is highly selective. To evaluate the selectivity of GSQDs for ONP sensing, the influence of several electrolytes, vitamins and nitro-containing compounds have been studied, including Ag+, Ba2+, Ca2+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, Zn2+, Cl-, Br-, I-, SO4 2-, histidine (His), proline (Pro), serine (Ser), tyrosine (Tyr), phenylhydrazine hydrochloride (PHH), p-nitrobenzoic acid (PNBA), p-nitrotoluene (PNT), 2,4-dinitrochlorobenzene (DNCB), Fe3+, Fe2+ and Cu2+. When iron metal ions are added to SQDs, they interact with the electrons in the sulfur atoms, causing them to lose energy and jump to lower energy levels. When these electrons return to their original energy levels, they release light to compensate for thelost energy. This is how Fe2+ and Fe3+ cause fluorescence quenching in SQDs. The surface of SQDs is rich in hydroxyl groups. The surface activity of Cu2+ is strong, and it can effectively bind to the surface of SQDs in a hydrogen-bonded oriented manner. In addition, Cu2+ can burst the fluorescence of SQDs by changing the surface charge state, reorganizing the hydrogen bonds and affecting the internal structure of SQDs. The fluorescence of GSQDs was only quenched by ONP, while the other substances had no quenching effect, indicating that GSQDs were highly selective for the sensing of ONP (Fig. 8c). The effect on sensing when disturbing substances exist was further investigated (Fig. 8d). When disturbing substances existed, GSQDs still had similar responses to ONP, indicated that the sensor had good anti-interference ability for ONP detection. In addition, GSQDs had excellent stability and reproducibility as fluorescent probes. As shown in Fig. S1, GSQDs had outstanding photostability under different pH, time, temperature and salt concentration conditions. Additionally, we had compared the sensing result with previously reported fluorescent sensors in Table S2. The results of multiple measurements of the fluorescent probes all demonstrated that GSQDs could sensitively detect ONP. It proved the high repeatability of fluorescent probes.

Following this, the quenching mechanism of GSQDs by ONP was investigated. After adding ONP to GSQDs, no new peaks emerged in the UV–vis spectra (Fig. 8f), indicated that no non-luminescent ground state complexes were emerged among the GSQDs and ONP. It indicated that the mechanism was not static quenching [33]. The excitation spectra of GSQDs had a large overlap with the UV–vis spectra of ONP at 410–500 nm (Fig. 8e), suggested that the quenching mechanism could involve fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or inner filter effect (IFE) [34]. The fluorescence decay curves of GSQDs before and after the addition of ONP were further recorded (Fig. S2). After the addition of ONP, the fluorescence lifetime of GSQDs remained unchanged. FRET could lead to shortening of the fluorophore lifetime because of energy transfer [35], so the mechanism of GSQDs quenching by ONP was IFE [36]. The overlap of the UV spectra confirmed that the IFE occurred due to competitive energy absorption between GSQDs and ONP, and absorption of emission energy from GSQDs by ONP. It resulted in reduced excitation and fluorescence emission from GSQDs. Expression (2) after calibrating the fluorescence intensity signal provided further evidence of the occurrence of the IFE process. Where F obsd was the observed fluorescence intensity and F cor was the corrected fluorescence intensity after removing the IFE effect. Aem and Aex were the absorption values at the maximum excitation wavelength and at the maximum emission wavelength, respectively. s was the thickness of the excitation light, g was the distance between the excitation light and the edge of the cuvette, and d was the width of the cuvette. Calculations showed that the value of F cor/F obsd continued to rise, demonstrating a gradual increase in IFE action.
(2)
F
cor
F
obsd
=
2.3 d
A
ex
1 -
10
- d
A

10
g
A

2.3 s
A

10
- s
A
em

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of ONP in practical samples
ce:section.ce:para: Detection of the substance to be measured in the actual sample was the main way to prove the practical application value of the sensor. Dye wastewater and pesticide wastewater were selected as practical samples to study the applicability of GSQDs for ONP detection. The test results were shown in Table 1 . The spiked recoveries in dye wastewater and pesticide wastewater were 96.7 %–100.4 % and 98.1 %–101.1 %, respectively. The results demonstrated the potential of GSQDs for quantitative and qualitative analysis of ONP in real samples.

The current SQDs had the limitations of cumbersome preparation, long reaction time, needed for additional passivators and fluorescence properties. This work demonstrated a scheme by changing only the precursor amine substances for the synthesis of SQDs. BSQDs, CSQDs and GSQDs were obtained by using EDA, PDA and BDA as precursor amine substances, which interact with sublimated sulfur, respectively. The precursor amine substances could change the growth size and band gap energy of SQDs, resulting in a broader absorption and fluorescence transfer to the long wavelength emission region, leading to tunable fluorescence emission. The morphological structure, elemental composition and fluorescence properties of the three SQDs were studied by TEM, XPS, XRD and fluorescence spectroscopy. This synthesis method was easy to operate and enabled the production of multicolor SQDs in a low-cost and large-scale preparation. Ultimately, the excellent optical properties and outstanding down-conversion properties of SQDs were successfully used in light blocking, optical anti-counterfeiting and sensing detection. This work provided a valuable strategy for developing multicolor emission SQDs possessing outstanding optical characteristics. In particular, sulfur-based luminescent nanomaterials with excellent properties had potential applications in the fields of sun protection, anti-counterfeiting and environmental monitoring.

Xiule Wang: Experimental design, synthesis, characterization, manuscript writing. Fanyong Yan: Review the whole manuscript and Funding support. Ming Xu: Fluorescence data collection and analysis. Jin Ning: XRD data collection and analysis. Xin Wei: Review the whole manuscript and Funding support. Xinyi Bai: FT-IR data collection and analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51678409), State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes (Z1-201507), and the Program for Innovative Research Team in University of Tianjin (TD13-5042). We would like to thank the Analytical & Testing Center of Tiangong University for Transmission Electron Microscope, X-Ray Diffractometer, X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2023.09.140.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
